# Sir David Attenborough's 100th Birthday on the BBC - Everything you need to know

A slate of new shows feature alongside beloved landmark series in a week-long celebration of Sir David's work and legacy

![](https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/400xn/p0nj1mhf.jpg)

Published: 2 May 2026

It's Sir David Attenborough’s 100th birthday on Friday 8 May and, to mark the occasion, a slate of new shows are featuring alongside beloved landmark series in a week-long celebration of Sir David's work and legacy.

Here's just a taste of what to expect...

## Making Life on Earth: Attenborough’s Greatest Adventure

The team who created Attenborough’s Life on Earth explain how the iconic series was made.

In 1976, production began on David Attenborough's Life on Earth. No-one had ever attempted a natural history series on this scale before. This is the remarkable story behind one of the BBC’s most famous wildlife blockbusters. A three-year, hair-raising odyssey around the world, travelling to 40 countries, across a million miles, and filming over 600 species.

Making Life on Earth: Attenborough’s Greatest Adventure goes behind-the-scenes on this ground-breaking landmark series, featuring exclusive interviews with David Attenborough and other members of the original crew. With fascinating insights, they reveal the highs and lows of filming the series during a truly exciting moment in television history, when global jet travel and colour filming were still in their infancy.

Along the way, the crew encountered multiple challenges, including a coup in the Comoros, being shot at in Rwanda and threats from Saddam Hussein’s army in Iraq. Broadcast in 1979 and watched by 500 million people worldwide, it confirmed David’s reputation as the most successful and influential wildlife filmmaker of our time. His astonishing encounter with gorillas in the mountains of Rwanda for this series is frequently voted one of the top TV moments of all time.

- [Watch Making Life on Earth - Attenborough's Greatest Adventure on iPlayer and BBC One from 8 pm on Sunday 3 May](https://www.bbc.co.uk/programmes/m002vzjt)

In Wild Isles and Wild London, David showed us the remarkable wildlife dramas playing out in the British countryside and on the streets of our capital city. Now, in Secret Garden, he’s turning his attention to Britain’s backyards.

Over five episodes - set in five very different gardens across the UK - David reveals the lives of the often charming, occasionally daring, always secretive animals that inhabit the hidden world right on our doorsteps. Theirs is no cosy existence - even in these beautiful and seemingly genteel surroundings the rules of the wild still operate.

From pine martens in the Western Highlands to dormice in South Wales, swallows in the Lake District to otters in Oxfordshire and blue tits in Bristol, the series reveals not just a rich and surprising diversity of life but also how each species finds its own way to live alongside us.

Through meeting the gardeners that have created these wild oases, we discover how our nation of animal lovers and gardeners can do their bit to save struggling species. Eighty per cent of Britons have access to a garden, and together they cover an area greater than all of our national nature reserves combined - so what we do in our own backyards has an impact not only on the animals that live there, but also on whole populations.

You’ll never look at your garden in the same way again.

- [**Watch Secret Garden on iPlayer and catch the final episode on BBC One on Sunday 3 May from 6.15pm**](https://www.bbc.co.uk/iplayer/episodes/m002sthz/secret-garden)

## David Attenborough's 100 Years on Planet Earth

![Sir David Attenborough leans on the door of a jeep with binoculars in hand, on location while filming for Seven Worlds, One Planet at Ol Pejeta Conservancy, Kenya](https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/400xn/p0ngww48.jpg)

(Image: BBC NHU/Alex Board)

To mark Sir David Attenborough’s 100th birthday, Kirsty Young presents a ninety minute special event honouring one of the most influential figures in broadcasting and natural history storytelling.

Broadcast from the Royal Albert Hall in London, jointly staged and produced by BBC Studios Music Productions and Natural History Unit, and in partnership with The Open University, airs on his birthday, Friday 8 May at 8.30pm, on BBC One and iPlayer.

As it brings the nation together to celebrate and honour David’s groundbreaking career, David Attenborough’s 100 Years on Planet Earth will take audiences on an extraordinary journey through a century of exploration and discovery in the natural world, seen through the prism of David’s remarkable life and work.

The evening combines some of the most memorable wildlife moments from the BBC’s natural history archive, with live music drawn from David’s most iconic television series, alongside reflections from public figures and leading voices in conservation and wildlife filmmaking.

![Clockwise from top left:  Sir David standing in studio in a suit in front of a series of animal skeletons in 1973, in an orange jumpsuit experiencing zero gravity in The Living Planet in 1984, and sitting behind two wandering Albatross on Bird Island, South Georgia while filming for The Living Planet in 1984.](https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/400xn/p0ngzxn2.jpg)

Clockwise from top left: In studio in 1973, filming The Living Planet in 1984. (Images: BBC)

As the celebrations unfold, Kirsty will be joined on stage by those who have worked with David and been inspired by his work over the years, including broadcasters Liz Bonnin, Steve Backshall, Chris Packham, and Michael Palin, who will reflect on the impact of David’s work, the legacy he continues to shape and his unique ability to bring the wonders of the natural world into people’s homes.

Accompanied throughout by the BBC Concert Orchestra, the programme will feature iconic music from landmark series including [Planet Earth](https://www.bbc.co.uk/iplayer/episodes/b006mywy/planet-earth), [Blue Planet](https://www.bbc.co.uk/iplayer/episodes/b008044n/the-blue-planet) and [Frozen Planet](https://www.bbc.co.uk/iplayer/episodes/b00mfl7n/frozen-planet). The orchestra will perform scores associated with some of television’s most unforgettable sequences, including the dramatic snakes and iguanas chase from [Planet Earth II](https://www.bbc.co.uk/iplayer/episodes/p02544td/planet-earth-ii) and the powerful wave-washing orcas sequence from [Frozen Planet II](https://www.bbc.co.uk/iplayer/episodes/p0cw0nb8/frozen-planet-ii).

The evening will also feature special performances from music artists who have collaborated on these series. Dan Smith, frontman of Brit Award winning band Bastille, will join the BBC Concert Orchestra to perform the band’s famous track Pompeii, featured in [Planet Earth III](https://www.bbc.co.uk/iplayer/episodes/p0gjwxhv/planet-earth-iii), while Icelandic band Sigur Rós will perform Hoppípolla. The track was used in the promotion of Planet Earth and Planet Earth II.

Other musical highlights include Sienna Spiro, one of the most exciting new voices in British music, and Paraguayan harpist Francisco Yglesia, who will play the traditional Pajaro Campana, a piece that featured in [Zoo Quest](https://www.bbc.co.uk/iplayer/episodes/p00db1jf/zoo-quest).

Bringing together breathtaking wildlife imagery, live orchestral and contemporary music, and contributions from those who have worked alongside David throughout his career and those who feel passionately about the natural world, the programme celebrates not only a broadcasting legend, but a century long relationship between audiences and nature.

- [**Watch David Attenborough’s 100 Years on Planet Earth on iPlayer and BBC One from 8.30pm on Friday 8 May**](https://www.bbc.co.uk/programmes/m002w132)

[Explore the Attenborough Archive\\
\\
**Attenborough at 100 on BBC iPlayer**](https://www.bbc.co.uk/iplayer/group/p03szck8)

Step into the natural world with Sir David Attenborough as your guide - extraordinary wildlife, stunning landscapes, and nature’s most inspiring stories

A stunning World Snooker Championship is in the history books after Wu Yize win a remarkable final 18-17 at the Crucible.

After a gripping journey through the tournament so far, Wu saw off 2005 champion Shaun Murphy in a quest to win the trophy held by Zhao Xintong.

There was more than just prestige on the line, though, as Wu pocketed a £500,000 cheque -- with more than £2 million available in the total prize fund.

**\- [Follow the World Snooker Championship final live with ESPN](https://www.espn.com/snooker/story/_/id/48666142/world-snooker-championship-2026-final-shaun-murphy-wu-yize-latest-updates-commentary-score-result-day-one-day-two)**

* * *

## World Snooker Championship dates, venue

As has been the case every year since 1977, the tournament is being played at the Crucible Theatre -- and it won't be going anywhere soon. A deal was struck last month to [extend the venue's deal until at least 2045](https://www.espn.com/snooker/story/_/id/48295910/snooker-world-championship-remain-crucible-least-2045), ending speculation the World Championship may leave its spiritual home.

**Where:** Crucible Theatre, Sheffield

**When:** April 18 to May 4, 2026

* * *

## World Snooker Championship schedule, draw, times

The first-round draw took place at 8.45 a.m. on Thursday, April 16. It followed the final round of qualifying matches -- known as 'Judgement Day' -- on April 14 and 15 where the final 16 players secured their participation in the tournament.

* * *

## What is the prize money?

- **Last 112:** £5,000

- **Last 80:** £10,000

- **Last 48:** £15,000

- **Last 32:** £20,000

- **Last 16:** £30,000

- **Quarterfinals:** £50,000

- **Semifinals:** £100,000

- **Runner-up:** £200,000

- **Winner:** £500,000

- **High break:** £15,000

- **Total:** _£2,395,000_


* * *

## World Snooker Championship format

The World Championship is a straight knockout tournament with 32 players competing across 17 days at the Crucible. The field is split between the world's top 16 players, who qualify automatically based on a ranking of the 2025-26 snooker season, and 16 others who must battle their way through the qualifying rounds in Sheffield.

Zhao Xintong became just the third player to win as a qualifier last year -- meaning he played four more matches than runner-up Mark Williams before they met in the final.

The number of frames per match increases throughout the tournament, starting as a best-of-19 contest in the first round to best-of-33 in the final.

* * *

## How to watch the 2026 World Snooker Championship

The tournament will be shown live in the UK on the BBC and on TNT Sports. The BBC will be showing matches on BBC One, BBC Two and BBC Four, with every match being streamed on BBC iPlayer.

TNT Sports will also show every day of the tournament on TV, with streaming available through HBO Max.

* * *

## Previous World Snooker Championship winners

There have been 24 different world champions since the World Championship set up permanent home at the Crucible in 1977.

2025: **Zhao Xintong** 18-12 Mark Williams

2024: **Kyren Wilson** 18-14 Jak Jones

2023: **Luca Brecel** 18-15 Mark Selby

2022: **Ronnie O'Sullivan** 18-13 Judd Trump

2021: **Mark Selby** 18-15 Shaun Murphy

2020: **Ronnie O'Sullivan** 18-8 Kyren Wilson

2019: **Judd Trump** 18-9 John Higgins

# Hantavirus may have spread between passengers on cruise ship, WHO says

1 hour ago

Watch: Inside the cruise ship at the centre of the hantavirus outbreak

The World Health Organization says there may have been rare human-to-human transmission of hantavirus on the Dutch cruise ship where three passengers have died.

The virus is usually spread from rodents, but the WHO said in this instance it could have spread among "really close contacts" aboard the MV Hondius vessel. It stressed the risk to the public was low.

Two crew members - one British and one Dutch - are due to be medically evacuated by aircraft to the Netherlands after displaying "acute respiratory symptoms", the ship's operator Oceanwide Expeditions said. A person linked to a German national who died is also due to be evacuated.

MV Hondius set sail from Argentina on its voyage across the Atlantic Ocean around a month ago. It is currently anchored near Cape Verde, off Africa's west coast.

Medical teams from Cape Verde, supported by the WHO, have boarded the ship to help with the suspected cases, spokesperson Tarik Yasarevic told the BBC. Testing is taking place for other passengers and crew members who are displaying symptoms.

Images taken from on board the cruise ship show workers in hazmat suits in a smaller vessel alongside.

Some 149 people from 23 countries remain aboard under "strict precautionary measures", Oceanwide said. Aside from the crew member due to be evacuated, there are 22 other British nationals aboard.

"We do believe that there may be some human-to-human transmission that is happening among the really close contacts," WHO official Dr Maria Van Kerkhove said.

She added that the WHO suspected the first person to fall ill could have contracted the virus before boarding the ship.

Seven cases of hantavirus - two confirmed and five suspected - have so far been identified, according to the latest WHO update.

The two confirmed cases are a Dutch woman, who is among those who died, and a 69-year-old UK national who was evacuated to South Africa for medical treatment.

The woman's husband also died but he is not a confirmed case, nor is the German national who passed away on 2 May.

[**Cruise passengers tell of life on board stranded ship after hantavirus outbreak**](https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/clype7ededzo)

[**What is the hantavirus that has been confirmed on an Atlantic cruise ship?**](https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/c8r8j1l6j0go)

![](https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/320x180/p0nj6q6v.jpg)

In a statement, the Dutch couple's family said: "The beautiful journey they experienced together was abruptly and permanently cut short."

"We are still unable to comprehend that we have lost them. We wish to bring them home and commemorate them in peace and privacy," it added.

Investigators are working under the assumption that the Andes strain of the virus, which spreads in South America where the cruise began, has been found in the two confirmed cases.

The organisation was told there were no rats on board, Van Kerkhove said, adding that disinfection was taking place on the ship and those with symptoms or caring for patients were wearing full personal protective equipment.

"Our working hypothesis is that there's probably a couple of different types of transmission that might be happening," Van Kerkhove told BBC Breakfast on Tuesday.

She noted the cruise had visited many different islands, some of which have rodents, which typically spread the virus through their faeces, saliva or urine.

![Map tracing the route of the cruise ship MV Hondius across the South Atlantic, with numbered points marking key events. The ship leaves Ushuaia, Argentina on 1 April, a first passenger dies on 11 April, the first passenger's wife leaves the ship at St Helena on 24 April and dies in Johannesburg on 26 April, and another sick man is flown to Johannesburg on 27 April, another passenger dies on board on 2 May, and the ship arrives at Cape Verde on 3 May. The route is shown with a red line, dates and notes in text boxes, and reference locations including South Africa, the Canary Islands, and the South Atlantic Ocean.](https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/1024x1024/p0hq72jn.png.webp)

The WHO said Spain has granted permission for the vessel to dock in the Canary Islands, where a risk assessment and further medical monitoring could take place.

But Spain's health ministry has played down speculation that it will take in the ship.

It said in a statement: "Depending on epidemiological data gathered from the boat as it travels past Cape Verde, the most appropriate next stop for it will be decided.

"Until then, the health ministry will not take a decision, as we have explained to the WHO."

A spokesman for Spain's health ministry told the BBC it had not yet received a request for the boat to stop at the Canaries.

However, Spanish authorities are prepared to take charge of the situation should that change, the spokesperson added. That would include providing medical attention, analysis and disinfection.

They would not say whether passengers would be allowed to disembark.

Though they cannot yet leave the ship, one passenger told the BBC on Monday that the mood on the vessel was "pretty good".

"Hopefully the other patients on board will be tested soon and then we'll know what's going on," the passenger, who asked to remain anonymous, added.

Another passenger, travel vlogger Jake Rosmarin, said in a social media post: "There's a lot of uncertainty, and that's the hardest part. All we want right now is to feel safe, to have clarity and to get home."

_With additional reporting by Pumza Fihlani_

news.snippet: The foursome formed an unlikely bond when they ended up sitting next to each other on a plane but it looked to be a short-lived friendship, until a viral...

![Two people hugging](https://s.yimg.com/ny/api/res/1.2/vZEZXmx8qR8LWmsWWViDEA--/YXBwaWQ9aGlnaGxhbmRlcjt3PTEyNDI7aD04Mjg7Y2Y9d2VicA--/https://media.zenfs.com/en/wales_online_892/01f7b5ed4fa6c7c7ebb7133e7eeba5cb)

-Credit:BBC

(BBC)More

An unlikely friendship between a group of Welsh nans and a professional wrestler has been reunited after an appeal on social media went viral. Rose Sulley, aged 74, invited her best friends, including Susie Matthews and Lynn Crawford, on a trip to Las Vegas to scatter her husband Pete's ashes.

On their flight travelling to Sin City from London Heathrow, they had a chance meeting with British wrestler DeReiss Gordon, who performs under the moniker, Man Like DeReiss.

Advertisement

Advertisement

Advertisement

Advertisement

He was travelling to the 'entertainment capital of the world' to perform on the card of an independent wrestling show, one of the very many which were held in and around the city whilst major promotion WWE hosted its annual marquee event, Wrestlemania, at Allegiant Stadium over two nights.

- [Brown waterfall seen gushing from sewer pipe into Welsh river in sickening scenes](https://uk.news.yahoo.com/brown-waterfall-seen-gushing-sewer-065233712.html)

- [Family of rats spotted scampering around Cardiff store car park](https://uk.news.yahoo.com/family-rats-spotted-scampering-around-113243019.html)


They chatted for hours during the flight and got to know one another, and Mr Gordon ended up inviting the ladies to come and watch him perform in the show - not thinking for a second they would actually show up. **_Never miss a Cardiff story by_ [_signing up to our daily newsletter here_](https://www.walesonline.co.uk/newsletter-preference-centre/?view=Solus&mailingListId=04b7baad-60af-4999-8f27-3ec3c1e7d11e&utm_source=solusarticle).**

But, to his shock, they did, and he took a photograph with two of the ladies when they visited his merchandise table after his match. They lost contact afterwards, before Mr Gordon was able to extend an invitation to the ladies to attend a show on home soil in the UK.

![Man with two older women](https://s.yimg.com/ny/api/res/1.2/LwCp1xsPENxH8kmcCnK16A--/YXBwaWQ9aGlnaGxhbmRlcjt3PTk2MDtoPTY0MDtjZj13ZWJw/https://media.zenfs.com/en/wales_online_892/17cf9d446813618d1aa0bc69626b3ecd)

Man Like DeReiss alongside two of the ladies -Credit:Man Like DeReiss

More

He launched an appeal via social media to track them down, and they have now been reunited on [BBC](https://www.walesonline.co.uk/all-about/bbc) Breakfast. The ladies decided to return the favour by showing Mr Gordon around [Cardiff](https://www.walesonline.co.uk/all-about/cardiff), taking him for a game of bingo, and a trip on the Skycar Cardiff bus.

Advertisement

Advertisement

Advertisement

Advertisement

Mr Gordon told the show he had missed the flight he was originally meant to get on, and thought he was going to miss being in Las Vegas to perform in the show.

He said: "It just happened by chance I was there on that seat on that day, it was just meant to be I think.

"When I met them I could tell there was a lot of personality, charisma. They weren't your typical nanas, and were the most hilarious people I've ever met.

"Just because we're from different cultures and ages, there was still so much to learn about these women, they've lived many experiences which I haven't got to do yet, and it was interesting speaking and learning from them.

Advertisement

Advertisement

Advertisement

Advertisement

"Everyone should just chat to the person next to you sometimes, it doesn't matter how old or young they are. At times it is a missed opportunity to get to know more people outside of your bubble.

"I thought, these girls have to come to a wrestling show. There's lots of independent wrestling all up and down the country in England and Wales and they'd never been to a show so I thought, we have to change that. I was wrestling at GCW and invited them to come down, and I didn't think they would come down, but they came."

The ladies said it was about "grabbing life and enjoying life", because "life is too short."

![group of people waving their hands in the air on BBC Breakfast show](https://s.yimg.com/ny/api/res/1.2/xkKV.D5MkpLh1Dwtej5ROw--/YXBwaWQ9aGlnaGxhbmRlcjt3PTk2MDtoPTYzOTtjZj13ZWJw/https://media.zenfs.com/en/wales_online_892/e5f0059d95defa104da4b707f4b3b482)

Lynn Crawford, Rose Sulley and Susie Matthews with DeReiss Gordon on BBC Breakfast -Credit:BBC

More

Mrs Sulley said her late husband would have found it "highly amusing".

Advertisement

Advertisement

Advertisement

Advertisement

"He would have said, 'I can't leave you for five minutes'," she said.

"He would have loved it.

"The chatting and comradery, it really uplifted me and it was what I needed at that time. It was great, it was fab.

"Who would have thought it. People don't talk today. They are on their phones, or they are texting, people don't smile anymore. We need to have interaction again."

Mrs Matthews said: "We stepped off the plane, went home, and were told there was 1.6 million people looking for us. Our feet haven't touched the ground. It's been absolutely amazing, when you think of the reason we went for.

"We had gone to the wrestling show and there was about 4,000 people there, and there was all these men with big beards and baggy trousers, and there was us in a row like Sex In The City.

Advertisement

Advertisement

Advertisement

Advertisement

"We really think we should be part of his tag team. I'm sorting out all the Lycra ready for us to go to his gym."

# Snooker latest: BBC apology as family of World Championship finalist in tears

## The snooker World Championship reached its dramatic climax on Monday evening, when Wu Yize secured victory over Shaun Murphy at the Crucible Theatre

- [Share on Facebook](https://www.facebook.com/sharer/sharer.php?u=https://www.walesonline.co.uk/sport/bbc-snooker-world-championship-latest-33891339&text=n "Share on Facebook")
- [Share on X](https://x.com/intent/tweet?url=https://www.walesonline.co.uk/sport/bbc-snooker-world-championship-latest-33891339&text=n "Share on X")
- [Share on WhatsApp](https://api.whatsapp.com/send?text=https://www.walesonline.co.uk/sport/bbc-snooker-world-championship-latest-33891339&text=n "Share on WhatsApp")

[copy link](https://www.walesonline.co.uk/sport/bbc-snooker-world-championship-latest-33891339# "copy link")

[Comments0](https://www.walesonline.co.uk/sport/bbc-snooker-world-championship-latest-33891339#comments-wrapper "Comment on this article")

[Sport](https://www.walesonline.co.uk/sport/)

[**Aaron Morris**](https://www.walesonline.co.uk/authors/aaron-morris/) Senior Sports Reporter

18:45, 05 May 2026

![Hazel Irvine looks on](https://i2-prod.walesonline.co.uk/article33891337.ece/ALTERNATES/s1200f/1_Johnstones-Paint-Masters-Day-Four.jpg)

View 4 Images

Hazel Irvine issued an apology(Image: James Fearn, Getty Images)

Wu Yize claimed [a stunning victory at the World Snooker Championship](https://www.mirror.co.uk/sport/other-sports/snooker/world-championship-final-yize-murphy-37111428) on Monday evening with [a nail-biting 18-17 triumph over Shaun Murphy](https://www.walesonline.co.uk/sport/uk-sport-news/snooker-final-result-wu-murphy-33887243), though it wasn't the only talking point from the night.

‌

The Chinese prodigy, [who relocated to Sheffield just six years ago](https://www.walesonline.co.uk/sport/uk-sport-news/wu-yize-shared-single-bed-33885615) to chase his ambitions of becoming a professional player, had never previously won a match at the Crucible before this year's tournament.

‌

Yet a remarkable run saw him overcome the likes of Lei Peifan, Mark Selby, Hossein Vafaei and Mark Allen to reach the final. While Murphy looked to recapture his 2005 glory days and secure a second world title, it was 22-year-old Wu who ultimately emerged victorious.

‌

His win made him the second-youngest player ever to lift the trophy - narrowly behind Stephen Hendry's achievement in 1990 at the age of 21 - and the second successive Chinese player to triumph at the Crucible, [following Zhao Xintong's heroics](https://www.walesonline.co.uk/sport/other-sport/zhao-xintong-wins-enormous-prize-31574278) last year.

[Steve Davis accuses Wu Yize of 'unforgivable' act in World Snooker Championship final](https://www.walesonline.co.uk/sport/steve-davis-accuses-wu-yize-33886694?int_source=nba)

[Chinese media fire fierce warning after Shaun Murphy heartbreak in World Championship final](https://www.walesonline.co.uk/sport/other-sport/wu-yize-world-championship-murphy-33887877?int_source=nba)

Despite the extraordinary achievement, several other incidents drew attention away from Wu on the night. Here, **Wales Online** rounds them up...

### BBC issue apology

[BBC](https://www.walesonline.co.uk/all-about/bbc) presenter Hazel Irvine [issued an on-air apology](https://www.walesonline.co.uk/sport/bbc-snookers-hazel-irvine-apologises-33887664) to Allen during the World Championship final. Wu had beaten the Northern Irishman in a thrilling semi-final clash to book his place in the final.

‌

![Wales Online Icon](https://www.walesonline.co.uk/assets/icons/wales-online.svg)

Keep up to date with the latest stories, breaking news and more with our WalesOnline newsletter

Subscribe

We use your sign-up to provide content in ways you've consented to and improve our understanding of you. This may include adverts from us and third parties based on our knowledge of you. [More info](https://www.walesonline.co.uk/privacy-notice/)

Yet things could have panned out entirely differently had Allen potted a match-winning black to secure a 17-15 victory and book his own place in the bank holiday weekend's final, rather than falling 17-16. As the BBC aired a highlights package from this year's tournament, Irvine found she simply couldn't bring herself to watch Allen's missed shot again.

![Mark Allen plays a ball](https://i2-prod.walesonline.co.uk/article33891335.ece/ALTERNATES/s1200e/0_Halo-World-Championship-2026-Day-15.jpg)

View 4 Images

A routine black pot cost Mark Allen his place in the final(Image: VCG via Getty Images)

Speaking on the BBC's coverage following the montage, she said: "Nicely done Steve \[Bracknall\] aka Chris McClure, stage name, really genius stuff there. I still cannot watch Mark Allen's missed black, I am really sorry Mark. But the grace in which he took that was extraordinary, wasn't it?"

‌

John Parrott then added: "Unbelievable. If you are watching pal, I am still hurting for you. I am absolutely am, I have never seen anything like it."

Although Allen was unlikely to have been reliving the agonising moment himself, he readily admitted his mistake following his defeat to Wu. He said: "You don't deserve to be in the world final if you're missing balls like that.

"That was just pure pressure to be honest. Normally I'm pretty good under the pressure but just didn't handle it well today. I was probably more nervous at different stages of the match than I was on that black.

‌

"I just threw a quick one in, I genuinely did. I just didn't deliver the cue properly and I got what I deserved when you miss a black like that to win the match. I had two, three chances to close out the match and didn't do it. But credit to Wu."

### Wu's family in tears

Wu also paid tribute to his [visibly emotional parents](https://www.walesonline.co.uk/sport/other-sport/world-snooker-championship-wu-yize-33887591) following his World Championship victory. When asked by Irvine whether he had any words for his mother and father, who had backed his move to a one-bedroom flat in pursuit of his dreams, Wu had to compose himself before responding.

After a prolonged pause, he said: "My parents are the true champions. Since I made the decision to drop out of [school](https://www.walesonline.co.uk/all-about/wales-schools), my dad has been by my side. My mum has also gone through a lot over the years, they are the source of my strength. I love them so much."

‌

![Wu Yize stands with his parents and the World Championship](https://i2-prod.walesonline.co.uk/article33891333.ece/ALTERNATES/s1200e/0_Halo-World-Championship-2026-Day-17.jpg)

View 4 Images

It was an emotional evening for Wu Yize and his family(Image: VCG via Getty Images)

Wu's mother was seen dabbing a tear from her eyes as her son spoke warmly about his family's unwavering support, while his father's expression was equally overcome with emotion.

### Murphy proven right

Gracious in defeat, Murphy heaped praise upon his opponent before noting that he had previously tipped Wu to one day lift the world title. He said: "I'd like to be the first to congratulate Wu Yize and his family.

‌

"And everyone around him for being a wonderful world champion. I hate being right, I said sometime earlier in the season, we had a great game out in China somewhere, and I managed to win that one.

![Shaun Murphy embraces Wu Yize](https://i2-prod.walesonline.co.uk/article33891336.ece/ALTERNATES/s1200e/0_Halo-World-Championship-2026-Day-17.jpg)

View 4 Images

Shaun Murphy was beaten by Wu Yize(Image: VCG via Getty Images)

"I came out afterwards and said that he would be world champion one day, it's just a real shame that it was today, but I couldn't have given it anymore. I couldn't have tried harder.

Article continues below

"I played the best shots I could have played and I didn't get my chance. I couldn't do any more than that."

# Snooker is still a jewel in the BBC crown - but there is a huge void that needs filling

## 22-year-old Wu Yize produced a sensational performance to become snooker's latest world champion but the home challenge relied on the home guard

- [Share on Facebook](https://www.facebook.com/sharer/sharer.php?u=https://www.mirror.co.uk/sport/other-sports/snooker/snooker-still-jewel-bbc-crown-37113386&text=Snooker%20is%20still%20a%20jewel%20in%20the%20BBC%20crown%20-%20but%20there%20is%20a%20huge%20void%20that%20needs%20filling "Share on Facebook")
- [Share on X](https://x.com/intent/tweet?url=https://www.mirror.co.uk/sport/other-sports/snooker/snooker-still-jewel-bbc-crown-37113386&text=Snooker%20is%20still%20a%20jewel%20in%20the%20BBC%20crown%20-%20but%20there%20is%20a%20huge%20void%20that%20needs%20filling "Share on X")
- [Share on WhatsApp](https://api.whatsapp.com/send?text=https://www.mirror.co.uk/sport/other-sports/snooker/snooker-still-jewel-bbc-crown-37113386&text=Snooker%20is%20still%20a%20jewel%20in%20the%20BBC%20crown%20-%20but%20there%20is%20a%20huge%20void%20that%20needs%20filling "Share on WhatsApp")

[copy link](https://www.mirror.co.uk/sport/other-sports/snooker/snooker-still-jewel-bbc-crown-37113386# "copy link")

[Comments0](https://www.mirror.co.uk/sport/other-sports/snooker/snooker-still-jewel-bbc-crown-37113386#comments-wrapper "Comment on this article")

[Sport](https://www.mirror.co.uk/sport/)opinion

[**Andy Dunn**](https://www.mirror.co.uk/authors/andy-dunn/) Chief Sports Writer

15:50, 05 May 2026Updated 15:51, 05 May 2026

![Shaun Murphy embraces Wu Yize](https://i2-prod.mirror.co.uk/article37111854.ece/ALTERNATES/s1200f/1_Halo-World-Championship-2026-Day-17.jpg)

View 3 Images

Shaun Murphy was beaten in a World Championship final frame decider by Wu Yize(Image: VCG via Getty Images)

Ahead of the final, the highest-rated frame of snooker in the World Championship, in terms of viewing figures, was THAT frame. The one between [Mark Allen and Wu Yize which lasted for over 100 minutes.](https://www.mirror.co.uk/sport/mark-allen-wu-yize-bbc-37100247)

‌

Former professionals and some current professionals considered the frame damaging to snooker's image. Many categorised it as a farce. But the viewing public loved it.

‌

When it was taken off BBC2, 1.4 million people were watching very little happen. Snooker on the Beeb has some serious pulling power.

Article continues below

Any sport televised live on terrestrial television gets good numbers and the World Snooker Championship is a jewel in a BBC crown that has sparkled less and less over recent years. And don’t forget, the BBC also has rights to the UK Championship and the Masters which, along with the Worlds, make up snooker’s Triple Crown.

READ MORE: [Ronnie O'Sullivan stepped in to help Wu Yize midway through World Championship final](https://www.mirror.co.uk/sport/other-sports/snooker/wu-yize-ronnie-osullivan-worlds-37112326)READ MORE: [Wu Yize reveals hospitalised mum's sacrifices which led to Crucible triumph](https://www.mirror.co.uk/sport/other-sports/snooker/wu-yize-mum-snooker-crucible-37112127)

They have those rights until 2032. Now that we know the World Championship is going to stay at The Crucible for at least another couple of decades, we can look forward to the usual coverage. In a way, that familiarity helps make the World Snooker Championship compelling.

And it is not just the familiarity of the surroundings. Fifteen years ago, the top six players in the world rankings were Mark Selby, Mark Williams, John Higgins, Judd Trump, Shaun Murphy and Neil Robertson. All six are in the top nine of the current world rankings.

‌

[![Daily Mirror Icon](https://www.mirror.co.uk/assets/icons/mirror.svg)\\
\\
Get More of Our News on Google\\
\\
Set Daily Mirror as a 'Preferred Source' to get quicker access to the news you value.](https://google.com/preferences/source?q=mirror.co.uk)

It is nice to see the seasoned players return year after year and you get used to their mannerisms, their styles, their interviews. Ronnie O’Sullivan is probably as popular now as he has ever been.

![Wu Yize kisses the World Snooker Championship trophy](https://i2-prod.mirror.co.uk/article37112324.ece/ALTERNATES/s1200e/0_Wu-Yize-kisses-the-trophy.jpg)

View 3 Images

Wu Yize won an epic World Snooker Championship final(Image: Getty)

But there is a Luke Littler-sized void waiting to be filled. In terms of popularity in the Chinese market, that sort of void has been filled by their magnificent players.

‌

Zhao Xintong, the 2025 world champion, and Wu Yize, who beat the wonderful Murphy in that epic final, could well be heralding an era of Chinese domination in the sport. There will be resistance from the old guard, that is for sure, but what the United Kingdom is crying out for is a young home favourite, in the style of Littler.

Liam Pullen, 20, lost to Chris Wakelin in the first round at Sheffield while 19-year-old Stan Moody looked like pulling off an upset against Kyren Wilson but surrendered a big lead. Moody, though, looks like the likeliest of the young players to make a household name for himself.

![Ronnie O'Sullivan of England chalks the cue in the second round match against John Higgins of Scotland on day 9 of Halo World Championship 2026 at Crucible Theatre on April 26, 2026 in Sheffield, England](https://i2-prod.mirror.co.uk/article37112325.ece/ALTERNATES/s1200e/0_Halo-World-Championship-2026-Day-9.jpg)

View 3 Images

Ronnie O'Sullivan has turned 50 but remains one of the most popular British snooker stars (Image: Tai Chengzhe, VCG via Getty Images)

Article continues below

Barry Hawkins is still in the world's top 10, having made his Crucible debut 20 years ago. He knows his sport is crying out for a homegrown young star.

Hawkins, 47, said: “Look what Luke Littler has done for the game of darts. Someone like Stan Moody could do that. He has all the talent in the world, is exciting to watch and if he went on to win the World Championship, it would be a massive boost to the game.”

There is an enduring popularity about snooker - and especially snooker on terrestrial television - that stalwarts such as Murphy, John Higgins and O’Sullivan, to name only three of many, deserve immense credit for. But the outstanding young Chinese players will need competition from these shores if the great tournaments that we can see for free are to flourish even more.

![Andy Dunn](https://i2-prod.mirror.co.uk/article2205697.ece/ALTERNATES/s338a/andy_dunn.png)

**Andy Dunn**

Andy Dunn is the Mirror's chief sports writer.
